title: Visualizing Vortex Cluster Dynamics in the Weak Type-II Superconductor CaSb$_2$
authors: Yusuke Iguchi, Nabhanila Nandi, Mohamed Oudah
abstract: Scanning SQUID imaging of CaSb$_2$ reveals dense vortex clusters with enhanced boundary susceptibility and suppressed internal vortex motion, which features inconsistent with both isolated vortex and flux tube behaviors. These measurements provide the first local visualization of magnetic dynamics within vortex clusters in a weakly pinned superconductor, offering a new route to probe non-monotonic vortex-vortex interactions that are typically expected in single-band type-II/1 or multiband type-1.5 superconductors. Although the superfluid density follows a single-gap BCS model and the Ginzburg-Landau parameter of CaSb$_2$ lies slightly outside the type-II/1 regime, vortex clustering and spatially inhomogeneous dynamics are clearly observed, indicating physics beyond existing microscopic theories for single-band superconductors.
summary: This paper uses scanning SQUID microscopy to visualize the dynamics of vortex clusters in the superconductor CaSb2. The researchers found that vortices within clusters move differently at the edges compared to the interior, showing enhanced susceptibility at the boundaries. This behavior suggests complex, non-monotonic vortex interactions that challenge existing single-band superconductivity theories. The findings provide a new way to probe physics beyond the standard Ginzburg-Landau description of Type-II superconductors.
main_problem: Determining the origin of vortex clustering in the weak Type-II superconductor CaSb2 and whether it arises from intrinsic non-monotonic vortex-vortex interactions or disorder-induced pinning.
main_result: The study reveals dense vortex clusters with suppressed internal motion and enhanced susceptibility at the boundaries, indicating collective dynamics that cannot be explained by simple rigid-body motion or independent vortex models.
method: Scanning SQUID susceptometry was used to simultaneously image local magnetic flux and local ac susceptibility at temperatures between 0.03 and 1.74 K.
model_or_system: The physical system is CaSb2, a weak Type-II superconductor with a non-symmorphic structure. The study uses a monopole model for isolated vortices and a linear superposition model for vortex clusters.
key_observables: Local magnetic flux (Phi), local ac susceptibility (chi), and the spatial derivative of flux (|grad Phi|).
important_parameters: Critical temperature Tc = 1.72 K; Ginzburg-Landau parameter kappa between 0.95 and 3.3; penetration depth lambda between 87 and 250 nm.
main_assumptions: The magnetic susceptibility signal is assumed to be intrinsic rather than an artifact of mechanical vibrations or magnetic-induced cantilever motion.
figures_summary: Figures show temperature-dependent morphological changes in clusters (from compact to fragmented), comparison of flux profiles with monopole models, and simulated susceptibility maps for triangular vortex lattices.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the material and problem, describes the scanning SQUID experimental setup, presents the discovery of cluster dynamics, tests various motion models (rigid-body vs. independent), and performs a validation analysis against extrinsic noise sources.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Scanning SQUID susceptometry measurements on CaSb2 with the applied field normal to ab plane yield a tem- perature dependence of the superfluid density consistent with a single-gap BCS model, showing clear deviations from pre- viously proposed multiband models [24].

FIG. 2. Local observation of partially collective vortex cluster dynamics. (a-b) Magnetometry Φ and susceptometry χ over an isolated vortex at 1.5 K in CaSb2, together with schematic illustrations (left). These serve as a reference for evaluating cluster behavior. (c–e) Left: schematic images of (c) magnetometry over a vortex cluster, (d) locally induced magnetic dynamics in a cluster, and (e) rigid-body-like collective oscillation of the entire cluster (shown for comparison). Right: corresponding measurements, (c) flux scan (from Fig. 3(c)), (d) susceptibility scan (from Fig. 3(g)), and (e) the derivative |∇Φ| calculated from (c), representing the rigid-body approximation mode. Scale bars:...

FIG. 3. Temperature-induced morphological transforma- tions and consistent dynamic magnetic responses of vortex clusters in CaSb2. (a-d) Magnetometry and (e-h) susceptom- etry of vortex clusters formed under under 1.7 Oe field cooling, shown at temperatures increasing from 1.3 K to 1.6 K. While the cluster morphology evolves from compact to elongated or fragmented shapes with increasing temperature, the enhanced boundary response persists, indicating robust collective vor- tex dynamics.

FIG. 4. Quantitative modeling of vortex clusters and dy- namics. (a) Cross-section of ϕ along the dashed line in Fig. 2(c) compared with a triangular-lattice cluster simu- lation Φsp, constructed by superposing isolated-vortex data with lattice constant a = 1.65 µm (range: 1.55—1.65 µm). (b) Cross-section of the measured χ compared with the scaled model α(χsp −χ0) + β|∇Φ| + χ0, with fit parameters χ0 = −0.12 Φ0/mA, α ≃0.17, and β ≃1.61 µm/mA. (c,d) Schematic susceptibility across vortex clusters in various su- perconductors, contrasting (c) strong-pinning and (d) weak- pinning regimes, with finite internal response appearing only in dynamic type-II and type-1.5 cases.
